title: Observation and Control of the Magnetic Photogalvanic Effect from Strongly Bound Excitons
authors: Xianfan Nie, Tarun Patel, Hyunggeun Lee, Chuangtang Wang, Meixin Cheng, Mingrui Lai, Bowen Yang, Matteo Pennacchietti, Shiyu Liu, Hechang Lei, Liuyan Zhao, Michael E. Reimer, Su Ying Quek, Adam W. Tsen
abstract: Photogalvanic effects arising from the quantum geometry of noncentrosymmetric materials are promising for next-generation light-harvesting devices that do not require a built-in electric field. Recent theories predict photogalvanic currents generated in magnetic systems with spin-dependent symmetry breaking as well as by bound exciton states, allowing for potential magnetic field control of the photoresponse and enhanced detection of deep sub-gap signals, respectively. We demonstrate the magnetic photogalvanic effect in a bilayer CrI3 tunnel junction with both magnetic field switching and electric field tuning of interlayer symmetry. By controlling for the polarization and energy of light illumination, we disentangle the shift and injection current contributions and find that the peak response occurs under resonant excitation of strongly bound excitons in CrI3. Our results can be captured within a many-body framework of the photogalvanic effect, while our devices function as tunable, multispectral helicity- and polarization-sensitive detectors that highlight the potential of 2D magnets for future optoelectronic applications.
summary: This paper reports the observation and control of the magnetic photogalvanic effect in bilayer CrI3, a 2D magnetic material. The researchers demonstrated that this nonlinear photocurrent is strongly enhanced and controlled by the resonant excitation of strongly bound excitons. The findings highlight the potential of these 2D magnets for next-generation, tunable optoelectronic detectors.
main_problem: The study aims to observe and control the magnetic photogalvanic effect, particularly demonstrating its origin from strongly bound excitons in magnetic materials.
main_result: A sizable magnetic photogalvanic effect was demonstrated in bilayer CrI3, showing a clear signature dependent on light handedness and the magnetic state, which is unique to the bilayer structure.
method: The research employed photocurrent measurements, varying bias, and using circularly/linearly polarized light, alongside TMR and RMCD measurements.
model_or_system: The physical platform is a bilayer CrI3 tunnel junction, which acts as a magnetic tunnel barrier. The effect is analyzed within a many-body framework considering strongly bound Wannier-type excitons.
key_observables: Photocurrent ($	ext{I}_{	ext{ph}}$), photocurrent MCD ($\Delta 	ext{I}_{	ext{ph}}$), and the dependence of these signals on light polarization angle ($	heta$) and magnetic field ($B$).
important_parameters: Exciton binding energies (e.g., 1.1 eV, 0.8 eV), magnetic field switching, and electric field tuning of interlayer symmetry.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the photogalvanic response is strongly linked to the excitonic resonances and that the effect is suppressed when inversion symmetry is restored (e.g., in trilayer CrI3).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the symmetry properties of the magnetic states, the device geometry, exciton peaks via reflectance, and the dependence of photocurrent on bias and polarization angle.
paper_structure: The paper systematically investigates the magnetic photogalvanic effect by first establishing the theoretical basis, then demonstrating the effect in bilayer CrI3 using polarization and field control, and finally confirming the exciton origin and layer dependence.
why_interesting: The work provides experimental evidence of a nonlinear optical effect (photogalvanic) whose mechanism is tied to many-body excitonic physics and magnetic symmetry breaking, which is highly relevant for understanding light-matter interaction in quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
